result:
The first thing that comes is memory of rain against a tin roof. Not a gentle drizzle, but the sort of storm where every drop soundses like a thrown coin, a silver insistence that the world still moves even while you sleep. I was five, wedged between a warm dog and a warmer parent; the sounds came clattering from far above, a metallic applause for nothing in particular. That roof is gone now, replaced by solar panels that hum instead of drum. The dog is gone too. The parent half gone, dialing in from the blank elsewhere of long illness. Yet the rain keeps striking somewhere in the mind’s ear, perpetual, recursive, rewriting its own score.

par

I learned early that language is a so-so raft in a flood. Words hold only because we agree precariously to let them. Say “tree” and someone, somewhere, summons bark, leaf, chlorophyll-curated shadow. But another might flinch—arbor as battlefield, limbs bulldozed, woodchipper on idle. Speak “home” and the map explodes with incompatible legend: mother’s perfume, mother’s curse, smell of rice rising, smell of mildew, keys that fit, locks changed overnight. The word doesn’t wobble, we do. The shell stays hard, the filling leaks.

Whenever the news rolls in like a smoke plume on an unlit road, I return to that thought: consensus is a sleight of hand. We read the same line—twelve dead, market down, cease-fire signed—and each of us walks away carrying a different percentage. Some carry ninety-five parts outrage, five parts shame that it isn’t ninety-six. Others pocket the convenient fragment that exonerates a side, a self. The atomized lyrics of the twenty-first century: chorus of disbelief, bridge of rationalization, fade-out on a tiktok loop. We have never been more surrounded by information’s surplus camerawork and less convergent on any place roomy enough to grieve together.

Yet something stubbornly communal persists. The night bus at 3 a.m.; the driver humming an old love song neither of your parents knew they shared. Strangers are sometimes gentle in the exact shape your heart would never predict. A woman ahead of me once offered the last tissue in her purse to a coughing boy, and the light caught the small torn flap of the pocket pack—neon violet and plastic and perfect. There’s a holiness inside tiny economies of kindness. I want to write laws that weigh that tissue at triple the value of any unicorn startup. I want to pay taxes in laundered goodwill and receive refunds in glances unafraid of each other.

Often I think about how the brain mistook evolution’s rough draft for final edition. We got architecture for lightning-fast threat detection, but the footnote about long-term equanimity arrived in micro-font on the back page. We smell smoke where there is no fire, and sometimes miss the fire because everyone is busy arguing about what kind of smoke it is. Apocalypse as poor product design. Meanwhile my neighbors put up purple holiday lights in April “just because.” They string them across a balcony framed by dying cedar siding, broadcasting softness to anyone who remembers to look up. Electric valentines for an entire species that forgot to read the room.

My brother still texts me every time he hears cicadas beginning to rasp in overlapping arcs, a Morse code of summer coming. He grew up inland where the heat hoards itself under car seats and sidewalks, and those insects were our announcement that school was almost done, freedom was mostly nonexistent, but had altered its costume from plaid to cutoffs. Thirty years later he records the buzz on his phone and beams it thousands of miles to me—a sibling baton pass across fiber optic night. The sound arrives compressed, barely more than ghost scratch, yet the moment I press play my mouth floods with nucleotide memory of popsicles that dyed tongues radioactive orange. I have never solved the physics by which a sound shorter than two seconds can haul crates of retroactive temperature, scent, sunburn nostalgia. Maybe the equation is love times time divided by attenuated bandwidth. Maybe it’s just Tuesdays refusing calculus, turning into embodied poetry when no one is looking.

There’s a woman in my town who plants nasturtiums in sidewalk cracks. Violent little sunbursts of orange and yellow leaping out of concrete like kidnapped sunsets. She refused a newspaper interview—“I’m busy, talk to the seeds.” Their tendrils curl along fissures so persistently that the city maintenance crew gave up patching that block. The official explanation is “budget reallocation,” but everyone knows the flowers won a quiet revolution. Occasional petals drift into the bike lane; riders pass over them, emerge with specks of color stuck in tire tread, spreading overnight confetti as far as the bridge and back. For weeks whole streets exude a mild perfume of pepper and honey. Then the blooms finish their sentence and retreat into compost anonymity. But an underground map flickers in collective memory: You were here when color demanded its paragraph in the municipal ledger.

I dream in languages that do not exist. A tongue where verbs for turning toward someone and away from someone are conjugated identically, distinguished only by a pause as fragile as the hush after snow becomes snow. Another where “future” is conjugated in second person plural exclusively: a future comes only for “us,” never for “I.” In the dream I speak fluently until waking, when in the hush between clock ticks I retain just a fleck—some single syllable that sounded like a coin rolling on edge then falling into the soft throat of a lake. Gone. I can’t prove it ever was, yet the ghost of articulation lingers beneath my tongue, a phantom consonant pressing for visa renewal at the border of the mouth.

We are instructed to believe meaning must be durable. Monumental stone, chiseled intent, preservation teams on elevators polishing the foreheads of statues no one asked for. But monuments are merely calendars we forgot how to read. A shard of pottery from the backyard tells larger truths than the bronze general frozen mid-gesture: it once held soup, watered a child, was entrusted to carry heat and failed and shattered and got reused as the very bottom layer of a privy wall and still carries a thumbprint. That disrespected history is, to me, more reverent than any victory column. Messy continuity is the highest fidelity.

Once, art class, the assignment was “make something that survives forgetting.” A boy painted his name so small on a grain of rice that a second grain could eclipse it. Teacher frowned. Another child recorded the sound of her swallowing water and looped it for three minutes, labeled the file thirst.mp3, uploaded it to a stream where it would be buried under sequel trailers in six days. We grow small or fast as the medium demands because we know that forgetting is a determined editor with an exquisite red pen. What survives is not size or permanence; it’s the curve at which a heart bends to listen. A drift of pollen can endure long after marble has grieved itself to sand.

If I could gift you anything on this random page among unnumbered pages it would be the sudden, reckless assurance that your private astonishments belong. The way you notice dusk fingering every corner of the ceiling as if looking for a switch nobody installed. The way you count stairs when anxious and always land on thirteen even when the building architect swore there were twelve. The way you kept the broken watch of your grandparent not because it tells time but because at 4:22 its hands form a tiny mountain ridge identical to the one you hiked before receiving the call that they’d died. These might seem like thin threads. They are the actual rope. Weave enough frays together and someone somewhere uses it to swing across a ravine we will never see.

We are unfinished translations of each other’s emergency exit maps. Every conversation is rehearsal for a disaster that might never come, and still kindness means pointing out the dim green glow of the doorway before the smoke arrives. No version of this story ends with everyone saved. Some end with everyone known. That, too, is triumph.

I am down now to fewer words than fingers. If you listen, somewhere a roof of tin might be getting its rehearsal tonight, rain bruising the metal like a drummer who has forgiven time. I hope you are near enough to such music. I hope when the coins finish falling you discover in the darkness a small pile of accepted currency, shiny enough to trade for softer days.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
